planting.note: Plant balled-and-burlapped or container-grown trees in spring or fall in moist, well-drained soil; Eastern redbud transplants best when young (4–6 feet) and resents being moved once established. Site in part shade in hot climates — afternoon shade is essential in zones 8–9 to prevent leaf scorch.

directions: Plant container-grown trees in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in part shade to full sun; Eastern redbud is one of North America's most beloved native understory trees, erupting each spring in clouds of vivid magenta-pink flowers that cover the bare branches for two weeks before the heart-shaped leaves emerge — one of the most spectacular floral displays of any temperate tree. The flat, pea-like flowers are edible and can be added to salads, pickled in vinegar, or used as a colorful garnish. After flowering, the attractive rounded to heart-shaped leaves provide clean summer shade, turning golden-yellow in fall. The species is native from New Jersey to Nebraska and south to northern Florida, making it well-adapted to the Gulf Coast from zone 7 through 9 with afternoon shade; 'Oklahoma' and 'Texas White' are compact heat-tolerant cultivars excellent for the South. The weeping form 'Traveller' and the dark-foliaged 'Forest Pansy' are outstanding landscape selections.

planting.note: Root softwood cuttings in summer or plant container-grown shrubs in spring; Virginia sweetspire is notably adaptable and tolerates poorly drained, wet soils where most shrubs fail. It spreads slowly by root suckers to form loose colonies and can be divided in early spring.

directions: Plant in moist to wet, humus-rich, slightly acidic soil in part shade to full shade; Virginia sweetspire is an outstanding native shrub for difficult moist or shaded sites, producing long (2–6 inch) arching spikes of fragrant white flowers in early summer and then delivering some of the richest fall foliage of any native shrub — leaves turn crimson, scarlet, orange, and burgundy-purple and often hold into early winter long after most deciduous shrubs have dropped their leaves. The multi-stem arching habit is graceful year-round. Unlike many shade shrubs, it tolerates both periodically wet conditions and drought once established, making it excellent for rain gardens, woodland edges, and stream banks. 'Henry's Garnet' is the most widely grown cultivar, with outstanding wine-red to deep scarlet fall color and somewhat larger flower spikes than the species.

planting.note: Plant container-grown shrubs in spring or fall; arrowwood viburnum is highly adaptable to a wide range of soil conditions from dry to moist and pH from slightly acidic to alkaline. Plant two different clones nearby for reliable fruit set, as cross-pollination improves berry production significantly.

directions: Plant in moist, well-drained to average soil in part shade to full sun; arrowwood viburnum is one of the most adaptable and wildlife-valuable native shrubs of eastern North America, producing flat-topped clusters of small white flowers in late spring that attract butterflies and native bees in abundance, followed by clusters of metallic blue-black berries in late summer that are among the most preferred fruits of over 35 species of birds including bluebirds, robins, catbirds, cedar waxwings, and wood thrushes. The glossy, coarsely-toothed leaves turn orange-red to purple in fall, making it a multi-season performer. It was named 'arrowwood' because Native Americans used the straight, stiff young stems for arrow shafts. The multi-stem rounded habit is excellent for hedges, shrub borders, and naturalistic plantings. Extremely cold-hardy and adaptable to clay soils.

planting.note: Plant container-grown shrubs in spring in acid, well-drained, humus-rich soil; mountain laurel requires soil pH of 4.5–5.5 and excellent drainage — soggy soils are fatal. Amend with peat moss or pine bark. Mulch heavily with pine bark or pine straw to maintain moisture and acidity. All parts are toxic if ingested.

directions: Plant in acidic, well-drained, humus-rich soil in part shade, avoiding afternoon sun in hot climates; mountain laurel is the state flower of Connecticut and Pennsylvania and arguably the most spectacular native flowering shrub in the eastern United States, producing dense clusters (corymbs) of intricate, bowl-shaped flowers in white, pink, deep pink, red, or bicolored patterns with contrasting star-like markings in late spring — each bud is geometrically folded like an origami flower before opening. The evergreen, lance-shaped leaves provide year-round interest and the gnarled, multi-stem growth habit becomes increasingly sculptural with age. It thrives in the dappled shade of pine and oak forests and is essential to the native woodland garden. Over 80 named cultivars offer great variation in flower color and plant size from dwarf (2 feet) to large (12 feet). All plant parts contain grayanotoxins and are toxic to humans and livestock.

planting.note: Plant container-grown specimens in moist, well-drained, humus-rich, slightly acidic soil in part to full shade; bottlebrush buckeye is one of the few large shrubs that genuinely thrives in deep shade. It spreads by root suckers to form colonies — allow adequate space of 8–12 feet diameter per plant.

directions: Plant in moist, fertile, humus-rich soil in deep to part shade; bottlebrush buckeye is one of the most dramatic large shade shrubs in American horticulture, producing spectacular 8–12 inch cylindrical flower spikes (panicles) of densely packed white flowers with long, prominent, reddish-pink stamens in midsummer — hence 'bottlebrush' — at precisely the time when most shade shrubs have finished blooming, filling a critical gap in the summer woodland garden. The bold, tropical-looking, palmately compound leaves (5–7 leaflets each to 8 inches long) create dense shade and turn clear yellow in fall. Native to the understory of moist forests in Alabama and Georgia, it is one of the rare large shrubs that performs as well or better in dense shade as in sun. Spreads by suckers to form broad, dramatic colonies. Hummingbirds and large bumblebees are the primary pollinators. All parts are mildly toxic if ingested.
